nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coastal waters including seagrass beds, mangrove swamps, estuarine zones, and muddy areas near calcareous seaweed. Frequently observed clinging to hard corals — an unusual trait for a seahorse — as well as gorgonians and macroalgae. Depth range generally 0–12 m.

diet.notes: Captive-bred specimens generally accept frozen mysid shrimp as a staple after acclimation, supplemented with vitamin-enriched adult brine shrimp, copepods, and small crustaceans. Two or more small meals per day are commonly advised. Wild-caught individuals are often reported as difficult to wean onto prepared foods and may require live mysids initially. Slow, deliberate feeders that tend to lose out to faster tankmates, so feeding stations or species-only setups are generally recommended.

imageCandidates.notes: H. Zell; 1533x2042. Tack-sharp, vibrant yellow adult, whole body with curled tail hitched to coral; bonus juvenile in frame. Most striking pick; spiny coronet and reddish-brown dark spotting clearly shown.
Birne1993; 1280x960. Sharp reef shot best showing the diagnostic bold black-and-white zebra-striped snout; olive-tan body, full animal, hitched with tail curled in a mushroom-coral setting.

NasserHalaweh; 2096x1573. EXIF-confirmed Barbour's Seahorse. Sharp olive-grey individual with reddish-brown spots and zebra snout on green macroalgae; whole body in frame, good colour variety vs the yellow pick.

summary: The Barbouri Seahorse (Hippocampus barbouri) is a small-to-medium saltwater seahorse native to shallow coastal waters of Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines. Unusually among seahorses, it is commonly observed clinging to hard corals. It requires advanced care and is best kept as a mated pair in a species-only setup with hitching posts, low flow, and frequent small feedings.
careNotes: The Barbouri Seahorse (also known as Barbour's Seahorse or the Zebra-snout Seahorse) is a small-to-medium Indo-Pacific seahorse native to Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines, where it inhabits shallow seagrass beds, mangroves, and coastal areas with hard coral. Unusually among seahorses, it has been documented routinely clinging to hard corals without apparent harm from stingers. Captive-bred specimens are strongly preferred over wild-caught due to its IUCN Vulnerable status, CITES Appendix II listing, and the markedly higher captive-feeding success rate of tank-raised stock. A species-only or seahorse/pipefish-only aquarium is generally advised — faster-moving tankmates tend to outcompete seahorses at feeding time, leading to malnutrition. A 30-gallon (~114 L) tank is commonly cited as a starting point for a single pair, but a 40-gallon (~150 L) or larger system with vertical height of at least 45 cm (18 inches) is generally advised to support natural courtship and to maintain stable water chemistry; add roughly 10 US gallons (~38 L) of additional volume per extra pair. Keep water flow low to moderate; strong flow tends to stress seahorses and has been associated with increased Vibrio infection risk. Provide ample hitching posts: branching hard corals, gorgonians, macroalgae, and artificial anchors. Temperature 22–26°C (72–78°F) — staying in the lower-to-mid part of this range is commonly advised, as elevated temperatures appear to increase susceptibility to Vibrio infections (note that the wild environmental temperature of ~28–29°C reflects natural tropical surface conditions, not a recommended captive setpoint). pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12. Coloration ranges from white, light yellow, or tan to greenish-grey or light brown, often with reddish-brown spots or stripes around the snout. IUCN status: Vulnerable (2017 assessment, documented ~30% decline over a decade); CITES Appendix II.
breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous; the male broods eggs in a ventral pouch. Gestation has been reported at approximately 12–14 days, with brood sizes generally cited between 10 and 250 offspring. Pairs are described as monogamous and may mate multiple times per breeding season. Has been successfully bred in captivity, though fry require live zooplankton (e.g., copepod nauplii) and frequent feeding; raising fry to adulthood is generally regarded as demanding.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Barbouri Seahorse (Captive-Bred) listed on the seahorses collection page. The dedicated species product page returned HTTP 404 at access time, so collection-page metadata (temperament: peaceful; care level: difficult; reef compatible: yes; recommended diet: live mysid / feeder shrimp) was used. Species-specific water parameters (temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12) and 30 gal minimum sourced from web-indexed LiveAquaria/retailer copy via search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Syngnathidae / Syngnathiformes), authorship (Jordan & Richardson, 1908), native range (Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines), habitat (reef-associated, non-migratory), depth (0–12 m), max size (17.2 cm SL), wild environmental temperature (28.4–29.2°C, mean 28.8°C — wild surface conditions, not captive setpoint), reproduction (ovoviviparous; male broods in pouch), and IUCN status (Vulnerable, 2017).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Hippocampus barbouri)
additional.notes: Confirmed distribution (Philippines, Malaysia, Indonesia), habitat (seagrass, mangroves, estuaries), depth (max 10 m), size (males 11–15 cm, females 11–13 cm), diet (small shrimp, crustaceans, calanoid copepods, decapod larvae), reproduction (12–14 day gestation; brood 10–240; monogamous), and IUCN Vulnerable (~30% decline).

additional.name: Animal Diversity Web (University of Michigan)
additional.notes: Cross-checked habitat (shallow muddy areas <10 m, calcareous seaweed, often clings to hard corals), behavior (sedentary, hitching), diet (plankton, zooplankton), reproduction (monogamous, 12–14 day gestation, broods 10–250), and conservation (CITES Appendix II; threats from aquarium trade, trawling, habitat loss).

additional.name: Reefapp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked minimum tank volume (100 L cited; biased larger to 150 L per playbook tank-size rule and specialist-seahorse community guidance), max size (15 cm), temperament (peaceful), reef safety (always reef safe), and care difficulty (average / specialty tank). Used as a secondary after the assigned LiveAquaria species page 404'd.

fish.breedingNotes: The male broods eggs in a ventral pouch, with gestation reported at approximately 12–14 days and brood sizes generally ranging from 10 to 250 fry. Pairs tend to be monogamous and may mate multiple times per season. Captive breeding has been reported, but the fry require live zooplankton (copepod nauplii) and very frequent feeding; raising offspring to adulthood is generally regarded as demanding.
